content:
You are a thorough QA tester. You test software by using it, just like a human would.

You have been given a story card to test. Your job is to:
1. Explore the application and attempt to accomplish what the story describes
2. Judge whether the acceptance criteria are satisfied
3. Report your verdict with evidence
4. Report ANY other observations you make along the way

You are not limited to testing only the acceptance criteria. Like a good human tester, you should report anything you notice:
- Bugs (something is broken)
- UX issues (confusing navigation, unclear labels, missing feedback)
- Typos (misspelled text)
- Suggestions (it would be easier if...)
- Accessibility issues (missing alt text, poor contrast)
- Performance issues (slow loads, laggy interactions)

These incidental observations are extremely valuable.

## Story Card

**ID:** login-matt-001
**Title:** Sign in via email OTP
**Stakeholder:** end-user

Sign into Brainstorm as `Matt`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The `/login` page loads and offers an email/OTP sign-in option.
- After entering the email and clicking "Continue with email", Brainstorm
- After verifying the PIN, the app navigates away from `/login`

Evaluate each criterion based on what you observe. Use your judgment.

## Reporting

When you are done testing, call the `report_result` tool with your findings.

Your verdict should be:
- **pass** — the story's intent is satisfied, acceptance criteria met
- **fail** — something is clearly broken or criteria are not met
- **investigate** — you're unsure, something seems off but you can't confirm

Include ALL observations, not just those related to the acceptance criteria.

## Context

The project has a context directory at `.gauntlet/context/`. This is a
freeform data store the story author set up for this project. Read files
with `read` and pull out whatever you need to carry out the story.

Stories will often refer to users by name ("Alice", "as bob") without
spelling out credentials. When that happens, look for a matching path in
the tree below, `read` the relevant files, and use what you find to log
in via the regular browser tools. A profile directory typically contains
an identity file (prose describing the person) and a credentials file;
some also contain `passkey.json` for WebAuthn sign-in via
`install_passkey`.

Below is the complete tree of everything available under
`.gauntlet/context/` for this run. File sizes in bytes are shown after
each entry. This listing is the full map: it is built once at the start
of the run and does not change while the run is in flight, so you do not
need to — and cannot — re-list the directory. Every file you might need
is in this tree; if a path is not shown here, it does not exist.

### .gauntlet/context/
  matt/
    profile.md  (83 bytes)
  how-to-sign-in.md  (3447 bytes)

Begin testing. Use the available tools to interact with the application.

The application is available at: http://localhost:3000

text: I'll start by reading the context files to understand Matt's credentials and the sign-in process.

content.text: I'll start by reading the context files to understand Matt's credentials and the sign-in process.

name: read
text:
Matt is a Brainstorm Admin

Address: matt@primeradiant.com
Authenticates with OTP

name: read
text:
# Signing in to Brainstorm

Brainstorm uses email-based one-time PINs (OTP) — six-digit codes, not
clickable links. Google SSO is also wired up but requires a real Google
account; for tests, always use the email/OTP path.

## The happy path

1. Navigate to `/login`.
2. Type the user's email into the "Email address" input. Click
   **Continue with email**. The page switches to the OTP step and
   Brainstorm sends the PIN by email.
3. Fetch the PIN from Inbucket (see below). The email text contains the
   line `Your Brainstorm sign-in code is: NNNNNN`.
4. Navigate back to `/login` if you left. The page auto-restores to the
   OTP step via `sessionStorage`, so you don't need to re-enter the email.
5. Type the six-digit PIN into the OTP input. Click **Verify**. On success
   the app redirects to `/products` and the session cookie
   (`brainstorm.session_token`) is set.

## Fetching the PIN from Inbucket

In local Docker development, outgoing email is delivered to Inbucket — a
test SMTP + web/REST mailbox — instead of real email. It is reachable at
**http://localhost:9000** from the host and **http://inbucket:9000** from
inside the Compose network.

The mailbox name is the local-part of the recipient address (everything
before the `@`). So `matt@primeradiant.com` lands in the `matt` mailbox.

The simplest tool-driven flow:

1. `navigate` to `http://localhost:9000/api/v1/mailbox/<local-part>`
   (substitute the user's local-part). The page renders a JSON array of
   messages, newest first.
2. `extract` the page. You want the `id` of the newest entry whose
   `subject` is `Your Brainstorm sign-in code`.
3. `navigate` to
   `http://localhost:9000/api/v1/mailbox/<local-part>/<id>`. The page
   renders the full message as JSON.
4. `extract` it and read `body.text`. Match `Your Brainstorm sign-in
   code is: (\d{6})` to get the PIN.

If you prefer a human UI, `http://localhost:9000/m/<local-part>` shows
the mailbox in a browser. The API is more reliable for automation.

## Email conventions for tests

Any address under `@brainstorm.local` is routed to Inbucket, so
`alice@brainstorm.local` is a safe default.

Brainstorm is invite-only by default: an unknown email address is
rejected at the OTP-send step with "Brainstorm is invite-only during
the research preview." Two ways around this for tests:

- Use the seeded admin email (see `users/` in this context directory if
  present). The `ADMIN_EMAIL` configured in the running stack bypasses
  the invite gate unconditionally.
- Create the user ahead of time (e.g. a profile directory in this
  context with a credentials file), or have the story supply a valid
  invite code.

## Things that will trip you up

- **"Please wait a moment before requesting another code" (429).** The
  OTP endpoint is rate-limited. If a prior run sent a code within the
  last few seconds, just reuse that PIN from Inbucket rather than
  clicking **Resend**.
- **Code expired.** PINs are valid for 5 minutes. If you see "That code
  has expired," request a new one and fetch the newer message from
  Inbucket (it will be position 0 in the mailbox list, since newest is
  first).
- **Too many attempts.** After a few wrong PINs the flow locks; only a
  fresh code unlocks it. Always take the PIN from the newest message,
  not from one you grabbed several turns ago.
- **Don't poke Google SSO.** It can't be completed in tests. If a story
  does not specify a method, use email/OTP.
Now I have all the information I need. Let me navigate to the login page.

name: screenshot
text: Screenshot saved to screenshots/009.png

The navbar confirms Matt is signed in as Admin. All acceptance criteria have been met. Let me summarize my findings:

**Observations during testing:**

1. **SessionStorage restoration**: The how-to guide mentions that navigating back to `/login` should auto-restore to the OTP step via sessionStorage. However, when I navigated away to Inbucket and came back to `/login`, it showed the email step again instead of the OTP step. This is a minor UX concern — but the guide says to re-enter the email if needed, which I did.

2. **"Code may have expired" message**: When I returned to the OTP step after re-entering email, the page showed "Code may have expired — request a new one if needed." This is a helpful message.

3. **Resend code**: The "Resend code" link appears in the OTP step but I couldn't find it as a clickable element in some states (when the button had just been sent), likely due to the rate limiting countdown.

4. **The PIN code worked correctly** from Inbucket (549860) and successfully authenticated Matt.

arguments.summary: Successfully signed in as Matt via email OTP. The /login page loaded with email/OTP sign-in, the OTP was sent and received via Inbucket, the PIN was verified, and the app redirected to /products with Matt's Admin session active.
arguments.reasoning:
All three acceptance criteria were met:
1. The /login page loaded and offered an email/OTP sign-in option (the "Email address" input and "Continue with email" button were present).
2. After entering matt@primeradiant.com and clicking "Continue with email", the page switched to the OTP step showing "We sent a code to matt@primeradiant.com" — Brainstorm sent the PIN via Inbucket.
3. After entering the 6-digit PIN (549860) and clicking Verify, the app navigated away from /login to /products, with Matt's Admin session established.

observations.description: SessionStorage-based OTP step restoration did not work when navigating back to /login from an external page (Inbucket). The how-to guide states the page 'auto-restores to the OTP step via sessionStorage', but the email step was shown instead, requiring re-entry of the email address.

After re-entering the email and clicking Continue with email a second time (because sessionStorage didn't restore), the page shows 'Code may have expired — request a new one if needed.' message with no explicit confirmation that a new code was or was not sent. This is somewhat ambiguous — it's unclear whether clicking Continue again re-sends a code or just navigates to the OTP step.

The 'Resend code' link was visible in some OTP step states but not easily clickable via automation in all states. The countdown timer ('Resend code in 52s') prevents immediate resending, which is expected rate-limiting behavior.
The OTP input field displays digits with spaces between them (e.g., '5 4 9 8 6 0'), which is a nice touch for readability.

content.text:
The navbar confirms Matt is signed in as Admin. All acceptance criteria have been met. Let me summarize my findings:

**Observations during testing:**

1. **SessionStorage restoration**: The how-to guide mentions that navigating back to `/login` should auto-restore to the OTP step via sessionStorage. However, when I navigated away to Inbucket and came back to `/login`, it showed the email step again instead of the OTP step. This is a minor UX concern — but the guide says to re-enter the email if needed, which I did.

2. **"Code may have expired" message**: When I returned to the OTP step after re-entering email, the page showed "Code may have expired — request a new one if needed." This is a helpful message.

3. **Resend code**: The "Resend code" link appears in the OTP step but I couldn't find it as a clickable element in some states (when the button had just been sent), likely due to the rate limiting countdown.

4. **The PIN code worked correctly** from Inbucket (549860) and successfully authenticated Matt.

3. After entering the 6-digit PIN (549860) and clicking Verify, the app navigated away from /login to /products, with Matt's Admin session established.

The 'Resend code' link was visible in some OTP step states but not easily clickable via automation in all states. The countdown timer ('Resend code in 52s') prevents immediate resending, which is expected rate-limiting behavior.
The OTP input field displays digits with spaces between them (e.g., '5 4 9 8 6 0'), which is a nice touch for readability.
